alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeeplyTough is a convolutional neural network that encodes a three-dimensional representation of protein binding sites into descriptor vectors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeeplyTough, a convolutional neural network, encodes a three-dimensional representation of protein pockets into descriptor vectors for efficient comparison. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution but adds the purpose ('for efficient comparison'), a small clarification rather than a substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The network is trained to provide similar pockets with similar descriptors and to separate the descriptors of dissimilar pockets by a minimum margin. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The network is trained to provide similar pockets with similar descriptors and to separate dissimilar pockets by a minimum margin. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the training objective verbatim; there is no meaningful change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeeplyTough demonstrates excellent performance for held-out data coming from the training distribution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeeplyTough demonstrates excellent performance on held-out data from the training distribution and competitive performance on independently constructed datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the held-out training-distribution result but appends competitive-generalization results (originally P3), broadening the stated outcome.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained network shows competitive performance when required to generalize to datasets constructed independently. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeeplyTough demonstrates excellent performance on held-out data from the training distribution and competitive performance on independently constructed datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes the same claim about competitive performance on independently constructed datasets; it is restated alongside the held-out-data claim without narrowing or strengthening relative to this preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: peaBrain accounts for the majority (>50%) of variance observed in mean transcript abundance across most tissues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeaBrain accounts for the majority (>50%) of variance observed in mean transcript abundance across most tissues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative performance claim without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: peaBrain outperforms regularized linear models in predicting the consequences of individual genotype variation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeaBrain outperforms regularized linear models in predicting the consequences of individual genotype variation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance comparison is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The non-coding impact scores calculated by peaBrain correlate with nucleotide evolutionary constraint and are predictive of disease-associated variation and allele-specific transcription factor binding. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Non-coding impact scores calculated by peaBrain correlate with nucleotide evolutionary constraint and are predictive of disease-associated variation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the specific assertion about allele-specific transcription factor binding, narrowing the scope of the original multi-part claim while retaining the other correlations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tissue-specific peaBrain scores can pinpoint functional tissues underlying complex traits, outperforming methods that depend on colocalization of eQTL and GWAS signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tissue-specific peaBrain scores can pinpoint functional tissues underlying complex traits, outperforming methods that depend on colocalization of eQTL and GWAS signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this comparative tissue-mapping claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: peaBrain identifies putatively functional eQTLs that are missed by high-throughput experimental approaches. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeaBrain can identify putatively functional eQTLs that are missed by high-throughput experimental approaches. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same content about identifying eQTLs missed by experiments; wording and certainty level are effectively the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Positive functional connectivity was found between the claustrum and several brain regions including the insula and anterior cingulate cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific positive connectivity to the insula and anterior cingulate cortex; instead it emphasizes other network affiliations, so this specific connectivity claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings coincide with results previously reported in both animal models and studies with humans. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly claim concordance with prior animal and human studies in the same wording, so the preprint's explicit statement of coincidence appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The claustrum is evidenced as a node participating in different neural networks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The claustrum is a node involved in different brain networks. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same core conclusion that the claustrum participates in multiple networks; the publication frames it as a hypothesis but the substance is the same.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The functional connectivity pattern of the claustrum is different from the insular cortex's pattern. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: This specific comparative pattern with insular cortex is present in the publication but not in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The functional connectivity between the right claustrum and the frontoparietal network supports the hypothesis of claustral asymmetry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a lateralized (right-sided) connectivity/asymmetry result not stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings provide functional evidence that the claustrum is coupled with the frontoparietal network to instantiate new task states. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific interpretive conclusion about coupling with the frontoparietal network and task states that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: At least three brain networks related to the claustrum were identified in the study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The identification of a specific number of networks (>=3) is an explicit new result in the publication not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is an optimal size of a neural network for a given task, beyond which learning performance is impeded. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is an optimal size of network for a given task, above which the addition of neurons and synaptic connections starts to impede learning performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the optimal-size result equivalently; wording is slightly different but meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adding redundant neurons and connections beneath the optimal size can make tasks more learnable. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Adding apparently redundant neurons and connections to a network can make a task more learnable. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim that adding redundant neurons can aid learnability is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall brain size may be constrained by the need to learn efficiently with unreliable synapses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The size of brain circuits may be constrained by the need to learn efficiently with unreliable synapses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This interpretive conclusion is present in both versions with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some neurological learning deficits are associated with hyperconnectivity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some neurological learning deficits are associated with hyperconnectivity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this association directly; no substantive change detected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis uncovers fundamental relationships between learning rate, task performance, network size, and intrinsic noise in neural circuits. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis uncovers fundamental relationships between learning rate, task performance, network size, and intrinsic noise in neural circuits. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The stated contribution is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with schizophrenia exhibit reduced connectivity in the precuneus compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with schizophrenia exhibit reduced connectivity in the precuneus compared to healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this group-difference result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reduced connectivity in the precuneus is negatively correlated with the severity of negative symptoms, particularly apathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Connectivity in the precuneus is negatively correlated with the severity of negative symptoms, particularly apathy, in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The correlation with negative symptoms (apathy) is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings provide further evidence for the role of default mode network alterations in schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The current results provide further evidence for the role that alterations in the default mode network (DMN) might play in schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same interpretive conclusion about DMN alterations in schizophrenia with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NanoVar demonstrated the highest SV detection accuracy (F1 score = 0.91) amongst other long-read SV callers using 12 gigabases (4X) of sequencing data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NanoVar exhibits higher structural variant calling accuracy when benchmarked against current tools using low-depth simulated datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core result (higher SV-calling accuracy) but with more general wording about low-depth simulated datasets rather than the specific F1 and 12 Gb detail from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NanoVar uncovered many normal alternative sequences or alleles which were present in healthy individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NanoVar uncovers normal alternative sequences or alleles which are present in healthy individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this claim verbatim and with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The low sequencing depth requirements of NanoVar enable the use of Nanopore sequencing for accurate SV characterization at a lower sequencing cost. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the cost-enabling conclusion about low-depth requirements and lower sequencing cost, so this preprint conclusion is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NanoVar employs split-reads and hard-clipped reads for SV detection and utilizes a neural network classifier for true SV enrichment. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not describe these specific methodological details (split-reads, hard-clips, neural classifier), so the preprint's method description is not represented.

alignments.pub_claim_text: NanoVar successfully validates structural variants characterized in patient samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This validation-on-patient-samples claim appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ES-GC estimator shows a net advantage in detecting nonlinear, causal links compared to MVAR-based GC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Echo state GC (ES-GC) demonstrates a net advantage in detecting nonlinear causal links compared to traditional MVAR-based GC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the main methodological advantage claim using equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the existence of previously unknown directed within-brain interactions using ES-GC in a network of noisy Duffing oscillators. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reveals previously unknown directed within-brain interactions using echo-state networks and functional MRI data from 1003 healthy subjects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The finding is reported in both versions but the experimental system changes from simulation (Duffing oscillators) to large-scale fMRI data, which revises and broadens the evidence and likely increases confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ES-GC performs better than commonly used and recently developed GC approaches. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Echo state GC (ES-GC) demonstrates a net advantage in detecting nonlinear causal links compared to traditional MVAR-based GC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same overall comparative advantage; the preprint's broader statement maps to the publication's explicit net advantage versus MVAR-based GC.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current nonlinear GC estimators face considerable training difficulties and tailoring needs. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not reiterate this limitation about training difficulties and tailoring, so the preprint's limitation statement is not present.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Directed interaction between brain networks and central vagal cardiac control is explored in the context of the central autonomic network. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific exploration of directed interactions with central vagal cardiac control appears in the publication but is not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SPROF achieved 39.8% in sequence recovery of residues on the independent test set, representing a 5.2% improvement over SPIN2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SPROF achieved 39.8% in sequence recovery of residues on the independent test set, representing a 5.2% improvement over SPIN2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This is the first method to employ 2D distance map for predicting protein properties. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This is the first method to employ a 2D distance map for predicting protein properties. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is carried over exactly into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sequence recovery increased with the number of their neighbored residues in 3D structural space. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sequence recovery increased with the number of their neighbored residues in 3D structural space. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The network architecture using 2D distance map is expected to be useful for other 3D structure-based applications. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The network architecture using a 2D distance map is expected to be useful for other 3D structure-based applications. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This expectation/conclusion appears unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepGOPlus significantly improves the performance of predictions of biological processes and cellular components with F max of 0.47 and 0.70, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepGOPlus achieves an Fmax of 0.390, 0.557 and 0.614 for BPO, MFO and CCO evaluations, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (lower and more detailed) Fmax values across three evaluation categories, so the original numerical performance claims have been revised and overall confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepGOPlus is a novel method for predicting protein functions from sequence alone by combining a deep convolutional neural network model with sequence similarity based predictions. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed a novel method for predicting protein functions from sequence alone which combines deep convolutional neural network (CNN) model with sequence similarity based predictions. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the methodological contribution directly and equivalently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepGOPlus can annotate around 40 protein sequences per second, making fast and accurate function predictions available for a wide range of proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepGOPlus can annotate around 40 protein sequences per second on common hardware. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same throughput claim with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepGOPlus would have made it one of the three best predictors in CCO and the second best performing method in the BPO and MFO evaluations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This comparative ranking conclusion appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyRates is a Python framework that allows for the construction of a wide variety of neural models as a graph. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyRates is a Python framework that allows for the construction of a wide variety of rate-based neural models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the original, broader claim to explicitly refer to rate-based neural models, clarifying the framework's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model graph in PyRates is translated into a tensorflow-based compute graph for computational efficiency and parallelization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not mention translation to a TensorFlow compute graph, so this specific implementation detail is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The behavior of the model implementations in PyRates is consistent with the literature based on numerical simulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The behavior of the PyRates model implementations is consistent with the literature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this validation result with equivalent meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PyRates demonstrates computational capacities and scalability through benchmark simulations of neural networks with varying size and connectivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PyRates demonstrates computational capacities and scalability through benchmark simulations of neural networks with varying size and connectivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same benchmarking and scalability claims with the same scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: OFC neurons encoded the value associated with the single piece of evidence in the stimulus domain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The OFC neurons encoded the reward probability associated with individual pieces of evidence in the stimulus domain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result but specifies the encoded quantity as 'reward probability' rather than the more generic 'value', which clarifies the measure without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The representations of the value in the OFC were transient and the information was not integrated across time for decisions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The representation of the reward probability in the OFC was transient and did not encode the reward probability associated with the combined evidence from multiple stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim reframes the same transient/non-integrating observation in terms of 'reward probability' and explicitly notes lack of encoding for combined evidence, a clarification of the original wording without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of evidence was observed only in the DLPFC and only in the action domain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computation of the combined reward probabilities was observed only in the DLPFC and only in the action domain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same finding but specifies the integrated quantity as 'combined reward probabilities', a clarification of terminology with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Decision making in the brain is computed in the action domain without an intermediate stimulus-based decision stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The OFC and the DLPFC play distinct roles in the value computation during evidence accumulation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint made a broad claim that decisions occur solely in the action domain with no stimulus-stage; the publication instead reports distinct roles for OFC and DLPFC during accumulation, which narrows and weakens the original sweeping conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diffused neural system of Pleurobrachia consists of two subsystems: the subepithelial neural network and the mesogleal net with about 5000-7000 neurons combined. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The diffuse neural system of Pleurobrachia consists of two subsystems: the subepithelial neural network and the mesogleal net with about 5,000-7,000 neurons combined. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same anatomical estimate and subsystem description with only minor formatting changes in numbers; meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our data revealed the unprecedented complexity of neuromuscular organization in this basal metazoan lineage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our data revealed the unexpected complexity of neuromuscular organization in this basal metazoan lineage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens 'unprecedented' to 'unexpected', a tonal/wording change that reduces the strength of the claim while keeping its substance, hence clarified and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ctenophores significantly exceed what we currently know about other prebilaterian groups in terms of cell type specification and diversity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ctenophores significantly exceed what we currently know about other prebilaterian groups in terms of cell-type specification and diversity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same broad comparative conclusion with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The anatomical diversity of cell types in ctenophores includes at least nine broad categories of neurons, five families of surface receptors, and more than two dozen types of muscle cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The anatomical diversity of cell types in Pleurobrachia includes at least nine broad categories of neurons, five families of surface receptors, and more than two dozen types of muscle cells. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the taxonomic scope from 'ctenophores' (the phylum) to the specific species Pleurobrachia and reduces certainty from high to medium, thus revising and narrowing the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spiking models of neural network activity are sensitive to the integration step. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A network model tuned to generate specific oscillations is sensitive to the intrinsic properties of neurons and synapses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint made a general claim about sensitivity to integration step across spiking models; the publication instead emphasizes sensitivity to intrinsic neuron/synapse properties in a specific oscillation-tuned model, narrowing and refocusing the general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Larger integration time steps are preferable in simulating large networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An increase in the integration time step affects network behavior in a model of sharp-wave activity in the hippocampal area CA3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint recommends larger time steps for simulation; the publication reports that increasing time step affects (and, in context, harms) CA3 sharp-wave dynamics, which revises and narrows the prior recommendation and reduces its support.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A case study of CA3 sharp waves shows that an increase in time step damages network dynamics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An increase in the integration time step affects network behavior in a model of sharp-wave activity in the hippocampal area CA3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same CA3 sharp-wave sensitivity but uses the milder phrasing 'affects network behavior' rather than the stronger 'damages', a clarification with slightly tempered wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adjustments of neuronal and synaptic parameters can rescue the dynamics at large time steps. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Adjustments of intrinsic properties of neurons and synapses can help minimize or remove the damage caused by an increase in the time step of integration. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert that tuning neuronal/synaptic properties can mitigate deleterious effects of larger time steps; the publication frames this as mitigation 'help[ing] minimize or remove' damage and assigns lower certainty, hence clarified and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DNA molecular machines have great potential for use in computing systems. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DNA molecular machines have great potential for use in computing systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level conclusion about potential with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There has yet to be a demonstration of a fully integrated biomolecular computing system that has multiple levels of information processing capacity, can accept electronic inputs, and is capable of independent operation. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A fully integrated biomolecular computing system with multiple levels of information processing capacity has not yet been demonstrated. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation about the lack of a fully integrated, multi-level biomolecular computing demonstration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simulation results show that an integrated hybrid system could convert electrical impulses into biomolecular signals, perform logical operations, and take a decision while storing its history. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We present simulation results showing that an integrated hybrid system could convert electrical impulses into biomolecular signals, perform logical operations, and take a decision while storing its history. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes the same simulation result claim verbatim in content and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Theoretically, such a system could potentially be used to perform tasks such as controlling an autonomous robot navigating through a maze. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed system might be able to control an autonomous robot navigating through a maze. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication express the same speculative application to robot control with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transgenic mice expressing the sensitive red GECI jRGECO1a, driven by the Thy1 promoter, were successfully generated and characterized. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transgenic mice expressing the sensitive red GECI jRGECO1a, driven by the Thy1 promoter, were successfully generated and characterized. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The signal-to-noise ratio in transgenic mice expressing jRGECO1a is comparable to, or better than, that of mice transduced with adeno-associated virus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The signal-to-noise ratio in transgenic mice expressing jRGECO1a is comparable to, or better than, that of mice transduced with adeno-associated virus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about SNR is repeated without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thy1-jRGECO1a transgenic mice are useful for transcranial population imaging and functional mapping using widefield fluorescence microscopy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Thy1-jRGECO1a transgenic mice are useful for transcranial population imaging and functional mapping using widefield fluorescence microscopy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the practical conclusion about utility for transcranial widefield imaging.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Imaging of visual responses in retinal ganglion cells was successfully demonstrated using Thy1-jRGECO1a transgenic mice. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Imaging of visual responses in retinal ganglion cells in vitro was successfully demonstrated using Thy1-jRGECO1a transgenic mice. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the demonstrated imaging was performed in vitro, narrowing the original unspecified claim to an in vitro context while retaining confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PLSR models could reflect the relationship of FA content in both BCS and SCS and extraction conditions well. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PLSR models could reflect the relationship of FA content in both BCS and SCS and extraction conditions well. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is essentially identical between preprint and publication with no substantive change in meaning, scope, or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BPNN model was more suitable for TCS compared to the PLSR models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BPNN model was more suitable for TCS compared to the PLSR models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and certainty are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal extraction conditions for BCS and SCS were obtained by genetic algorithms, while those of TCS were obtained by particle swarm optimization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal extraction conditions for BCS and SCS were obtained by genetic algorithms, while those of TCS were obtained by particle swarm optimization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical methodological/result statement with no change in scope or reported certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are differences in the extraction conditions and compositions of FAs among different varieties of Coix seed. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are differences in the extraction conditions and compositions of FAs among different varieties of Coix seed. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion appears verbatim in the publication with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ASE combined with chemometrics methods can rapidly and effectively obtain the optimal conditions for the extraction of FAs from Coix seed. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ASE combined with chemometrics methods can rapidly and effectively obtain the optimal conditions for the extraction of FAs from Coix seed. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Contribution statement is preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The right inferior parietal lobule is involved in emotion and memory control, as revealed by meta-analyses of 95 fMRI studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The right inferior parietal lobule is involved in emotion regulation and memory control, as revealed by meta-analyses of neuroimaging studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication wording shifts to 'emotion regulation' and to 'neuroimaging studies' (broader than '95 fMRI studies'), clarifying and broadening the phrasing while retaining high certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This study identified a neural network and a set of genes associated with inhibitory control across emotion regulation and memory control. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Microtubules are probably not vital for the learning behavior in Paramecium caudatum. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of an antimicrotubular agent did not disrupt the learning process in Paramecium caudatum. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results call for a further revisitation of the microtubular information processing hypothesis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Psilocybin reduces functional connectivity within the ventral striatum in lightly-anaesthetized mice compared to vehicle-treated mice. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Psilocybin increases functional connectivity between serotonin-associated networks and cortical areas, including elements of the murine default-mode network, thalamus, and midbrain. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Psilocybin decreases functional connectivity within dopamine-associated striatal networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Psilocybin decreases functional connectivity within dopamine-associated striatal networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result is unchanged in wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interaction between serotonin- and dopamine-regulated neural networks contributes to the neural and psychological effects of psilocybin. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Interactions between serotonin- and dopamine-regulated neural networks contribute to the neural and psychological effects of psilocybin. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Information on molecular expression patterns and structural connectivity can assist in the interpretation of pharmaco-fMRI findings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Information on molecular expression patterns and structural connectivity can assist in the interpretation of pharmaco-fMRI findings. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some amygdala and hippocampal neurons are selective to multiple different unrelated face identities based on shared visual features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature neurons encode groups of faces regardless of their identity, broad semantic categories, or familiarity. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These neurons represent an individual’s face with a visual feature-based code rather than one based on association with known concepts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neurons in the human amygdala and hippocampus encode faces based on shared visual features rather than associations of known concepts. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feature neurons encoded faces regardless of their identity, race, gender, familiarity, or pixel-level visual features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature neurons encode groups of faces regardless of their identity, broad semantic categories, or familiarity. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The region of feature space to which feature neurons are tuned predicted their response to new face stimuli. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The coding regions predict feature neurons’ response to new face stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The predictive relationship is the same, but the publication expresses this result with higher certainty (medium -> high), amplifying confidence in the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results reveal a new class of neurons that bridge the perception-driven representation of facial features in the higher visual cortex with mnemonic semantic representations in the MTL. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results reveal a new class of neurons that bridge perception-driven representation of facial features with mnemonic semantic representations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication preserves the contribution while strengthening the certainty (medium -> high) and slightly streamlining the framing of the bridging role.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed learning framework based on a graph auto-encoder reliably assembles haplotypes and reconstructs viral communities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed learning framework based on a graph auto-encoder reliably assembles haplotypes and reconstructs viral communities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication text are effectively identical in content, scope, and expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm significantly outperforms state-of-the-art techniques in the reconstruction of genomic mixture components. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm significantly outperforms state-of-the-art techniques in the reconstruction of genomic mixture components. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is reproduced verbatim between preprint and publication with the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework is designed to exploit structural properties of sequencing data and to ignore sequencing errors. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework is designed to exploit structural properties of sequencing data and to ignore sequencing errors. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is unchanged in wording and intent between the two versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepACSON successfully segments large field-of-view, low-resolution 3D-EM datasets of white matter, capturing tens of thousands of myelinated axons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSON successfully segmented hundreds of thousands of long-span myelinated axons, thousands of cell nuclei, and millions of mitochondria. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepACSON accounts for severe membrane discontinuities in low-resolution imaging through its top-down design. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSON performs DCNN-based semantic segmentation and shape-decomposition-based instance segmentation for brain tissue ultrastructures. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSON performs DCNN-based semantic segmentation and shape-decomposition-based instance segmentation for brain tissue ultrastructures. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSON quantified the morphology and spatial aspects of white matter ultrastructures, capturing nanoscopic morphological alterations five months after traumatic brain injury. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A systemic age-dependent length-driven transcriptome imbalance disrupts the homeostatic balance between short and long transcript molecules in older organisms. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our LOF versus GOF prediction corresponded to molecular LOF versus GOF effects for 87 functionally tested variants in SCN1/2/8A and CACNA1I, with a ROC of 0.73. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction of T2D comorbidities utilizing Danish registers led to consistent albeit modest performance improvements over reference models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: NBBD is competitive with some of the state-of-the-art feature selection methods including Random Forest Feature Importance (RFFI) scores. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: NBBD is especially effective in reliably identifying IBD biomarkers when the number of data samples available for biomarker discovery is small. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: An integrative approach to Network-Based Biomarker Discovery (NBBD) combines network analyses methods and machine learning techniques for biomarker assessment. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several drug and hormone combinations were identified that could potentially be therapeutic for a higher proportion of male patients than SSRIs alone. (certainty=high, type=result)
