alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach provides a unifying perspective on joint dimensionality reduction and learning conditional distributions of neural and behavioral data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This method unifies dimensionality reduction and learning conditional distributions for high-dimensional multi-modal datasets in neuroscience.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same unifying perspective for high-dimensional multimodal neuroscience datasets, matching the preprint conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach was validated on a task with known ground truth.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit statement about validation on a known-ground-truth task that was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Encyclopedia of Domains (TED) describes over 370 million domains, which is over 100 million more than detectable by sequence-based methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using deep learning methods, we detected and classified nearly 365 million domains in the AFDB.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a similar domain count (365M) using AFDB/deep learning but drops the explicit comparison to sequence-based detectability, so the numerical claim is preserved while the comparative framing is removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nearly 80% of TED domains share similarities to known superfamilies in CATH, greatly expanding the set of known protein structural domains.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 77% of the nonredundant domains detected are similar to known superfamilies, greatly expanding representation of their domain space.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports essentially the same proportion (77% vs ~80%) and the same implication of expanded representation, so the claim is maintained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TED uncovers over 10,000 previously unseen structural interactions between superfamilies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We uncovered more than 10,000 new structural interactions between superfamilies and thousands of new folds across the fold space continuum.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly reports the same >10,000 new structural interactions and expands to also mention new folds, directly matching the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TED expands domain coverage to over 1 million taxa and unveils thousands of architectures and folds across the unexplored continuum of protein fold space.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We uncovered more than 10,000 new structural interactions between superfamilies and thousands of new folds across the fold space continuum.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 'thousands of folds/architectures' part of the claim but does not report the specific 'over 1 million taxa' domain-coverage figure, so the claim is narrowed to the fold-space discovery component.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database contains more than 214 million predicted protein structures composed of domains.
alignments.rationale: This factual contextual statement about AFDB size is present in the publication but was not listed among the preprint claims, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Amacrine cells show diverse chromatic responses in different spatial regions of their receptive fields and across the dorso-ventral axis of the retina.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 25 functional types of GABAergic amacrine cells with distinct chromatic and achromatic properties.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more detailed classification (25 functional types) that explains and expands on the preprint's observation of diversity in chromatic responses, broadening the descriptive scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Amacrine cells are less chromatically tuned and less likely to be colour-opponent compared to their excitatory inputs from bipolar cells.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly compares amacrine chromatic tuning to bipolar excitatory inputs, so this specific comparative statement appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A combination of pharmacological interventions and a biologically-inspired deep learning model revealed how lateral inhibition and recurrent excitatory inputs shape chromatic properties of amacrine cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lateral processing by inhibitory amacrine cells shapes chromatic information at the retinal output.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core mechanistic conclusion about lateral inhibition shaping chromatic information but does not detail the specific methods in the concise claim, so the result is presented more generally.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Amacrine cells balance the strongly biased spectral tuning of excitation in the mouse retina and thereby support increased diversity in chromatic information of the retinal output.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Amacrine cells balance the biased spectral tuning of excitation, thereby supporting diversity of chromatic information at the retinal output.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about amacrine cells balancing spectral tuning and supporting chromatic diversity with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Green and UV signals are primarily detected in the dorsal and ventral retina, respectively.
alignments.rationale: This specific observation about green/UV signal distribution across the dorsoventral retina appears in the publication but was not asserted among the preprint claims, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher-order patterns among excitatory connections are necessary for sustaining activity in sparse recurrent networks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results point to the crucial role of excitatory higher-order patterns in sustaining asynchronous activity in sparse recurrent networks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts necessity, whereas the publication frames the role as 'crucial' (less categorical), so the claim is revised and its confidence is slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cyclic transitions between specific triangle motifs are required for sustained spiking activity in the network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cycling of motif dominance generalized across manipulations of synaptic weights and topologies, demonstrating the robustness of this regime for maintenance of network activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes generalization and robustness for maintenance rather than an absolute 'required' relation, so the original requirement claim is revised and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The same network topology can exhibit either sustained or truncated activity depending on the initial membrane voltages. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulations run on the same topology exhibit sustained asynchronous activity under certain sets of initial membrane voltages but truncated activity under others. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same observation about dependence on initial voltages with essentially the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The robustness of the dynamical regime characterized by motif cycling is demonstrated across manipulations of synaptic weights and network topologies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cycling of motif dominance generalized across manipulations of synaptic weights and topologies, demonstrating the robustness of this regime for maintenance of network activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim closely matches the preprint's statement about robustness across manipulations; wording and confidence are effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CCTV operators can be reliably classified by patterns of saliency features and DCNN features compared to novices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a claim about reliably classifying operators by those feature patterns; instead it reports performance differences, so the specific classification claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CCTV operators show greater inter-subject correlation in attending to saliency features and DCNN features than novices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CCTV operators actively attend to visual contents with different patterns of saliency and semantic features than novices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same difference in attention patterns but does not explicitly assert greater inter-subject correlation, so the preprint's specific inter-subject-correlation framing is clarified/softened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The looking behavior of CCTV operators differs from novices by actively attending to different patterns of saliency and semantic features. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CCTV operators actively attend to visual contents with different patterns of saliency and semantic features than novices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim directly matches the preprint's description of differing attention to saliency and semantic patterns; content and scope are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Expertise in selectively attending to informative features at different levels of visual hierarchy may facilitate the efficient detection of social relationships and the prediction of harmful intentions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Expertise in selectively utilizing informative features at different levels of visual hierarchy facilitates the efficient detection of social relationships and prediction of harmful intentions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same hypothesis about expertise facilitating detection and prediction; wording and confidence are essentially unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The low-dimensional dynamics induced by low-rank connectivity structure are preserved even at high levels of sparsity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Low-dimensional dynamics induced by low-rank connectivity structure are preserved even at high levels of sparsity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about preservation of low-dimensional dynamics under high sparsity with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sparsified low-rank networks can support rich and robust computations even when connections are reduced to a biologically-realistic extent. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sparsified low-rank networks can support rich and robust computations even when sparsified to a biologically-realistic extent. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is carried directly into the publication with matching phrasing and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The eigenspectra in the complex plane of sparsified low-rank connectivity matrices consist of a continuous bulk and isolated outliers. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dynamics of low-rank recurrent networks with randomly sparsified connections exhibit a continuous bulk and isolated outliers in the eigenspectra. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same eigenspectrum structure; core content is unchanged though certainty in the publication is higher.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The theoretical framework of low-rank networks is robust to the sparsity present in biological networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Low-dimensional dynamics induced by low-rank connectivity structure are preserved even at high levels of sparsity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concrete results (preserved dynamics) that substantiate the preprint's broader conclusion about framework robustness; the claim is thus clarified by empirical results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Employing protein language models and molecular graphs as inputs to a novel graph-to-transformer cross-attention mechanism achieves screening power comparable to state-of-the-art structure-based models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work demonstrates that employing protein language models and molecular graphs can achieve screening power comparable to state-of-the-art structure-based models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same result about comparable screening power using the proposed inputs and architecture.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This novel approach allows for highly expedited virtual screening due to the greatly reduced compute required to run the model. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel graph-to-transformer cross-attention mechanism significantly reduces the computational requirements for virtual screening. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication claim substantial reductions in computational requirements enabling faster virtual screening; content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method enables the early stages of computer-aided drug design in the complete absence of 3D protein structure. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach allows for early stages of computer-aided drug design to be performed without the need for 3D protein structures. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's central contribution about enabling early-stage design without 3D structures; formulation and certainty are equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed transformers-based protein embeddings can quickly identify and rank millions of interacting proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed transformers-based protein embedding scheme can quickly identify and rank millions of interacting proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication duplicates the preprint claim about fast identification and ranking at large scale with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach outperforms existing sequence- and structure-based methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach outperforms existing sequence- and structure-based methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance superiority claim is carried over verbatim into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean AUC-ROC and AUC-PR of the proposed method is 0.73. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean AUC-ROC and AUC-PR of the proposed method is 0.73. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported evaluation metrics are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A decrement in learning performance emerged with increasing lithium doses in the reversal phase of the electric shock avoidance assay. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Increasing doses of lithium decrease learning performance in honey bees during the reversal phase of the ESAA. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same experimental result about lithium reducing reversal-phase learning performance with similar certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lithium did not affect the acquisition of the electric shock avoidance assay. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponding to absence of an acquisition effect appears; the published claims discuss the assay and reversal-phase effects but do not report this negative acquisition finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Api-TRACE is a computer vision-aided system that enables fast and detailed analysis of individual bee learning profiles. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Api-TRACE is a computer vision-aided system that tracks individual bees and analyzes avoidance assays. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same contribution but slightly reframes capabilities (tracking and analyzing avoidance assays) rather than emphasizing 'fast and detailed learning profiles', so the claim is clarified in wording but retains scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Super-resolution microscopy reveals and quantifies the nanoscale morphology of the primary cilium with a precision of 10-25 nm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Super-resolution microscopy reveals and quantifies the nanoscale morphology of the primary cilium with a precision of 10-25 nm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative microscopy result verbatim with the same precision and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Quantitative curvature analysis shows significant morphological differences in cilia upon impairment of intracellular cargo transport machinery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Quantitative curvature analysis shows significant morphological differences in cilia upon impairment of intracellular cargo transport machinery. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result is carried over identically into the publication with unchanged wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 2-color, 2D STED microscopy reveals abnormal morphology of the axoneme in mutant cells, correlating with changes observed in the ciliary surface. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cytoskeleton in the cilium exhibits abnormal morphology in mutant cells, correlating with changes observed in the ciliary surface. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same correlation but shifts wording from 'axoneme' to the broader 'cytoskeleton', representing a revision in anatomical framing while keeping the core result and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of 3D SR microscopy and computational tools can be useful for studying other biological or nanoscale structures of interest. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach can be useful for studying other biological or nanoscale structures of interest. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same general conclusion but reports it with lower expressed certainty (low vs medium), so the claim is effectively tempered in confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Shank3/F and Shank3/J models replicated some behaviors associated with autism spectrum disorder, such as social behavior and overgrooming, but these behaviors were milder than previously reported. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some behaviors replicated published findings, such as social behavior and overgrooming in Shank3 models, but these behaviors tended to be milder than reported elsewhere. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same observation about milder-than-reported behaviors but expresses it with somewhat lower certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Shank3/F model exhibited hypoactivity and anxiety-like behavior triggered by external stimuli that affected social interactions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Shank3/F model, and to a much lesser extent, the Shank3/J and Cacna1c models, showed hypoactivity and a general anxiety-like behavior triggered by external stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies and broadens the claim by noting similar but much weaker effects in additional models (Shank3/J, Cacna1c), expanding the scope while keeping certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No deficits were detected in cognitive procedural learning tests, nor was perseverative behavior observed in the studied mouse models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We did not detect deficits in a cognitive procedural learning test nor did we observe perseverative behavior in these models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this negative result with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differences in exploratory patterns of Cacna1c mutant mice suggest a behavioral effect in social settings. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this specific claim about exploratory-pattern differences for Cacna1c in social contexts; Cacna1c is mentioned in a different context but the specific exploratory/social claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Discrepancies between current results and previous reports may be due to variations in testing conditions, housing enrichment, or background strain. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this specific limitation text about causes of discrepancies; instead it offers different concluding remarks, so the preprint limitation was not carried forward verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Harnessing information from two different data streams improves the accuracy of inference in generative models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models that utilize information from both binary responses and continuous response times provide robust inference in behavioral data analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result about combining two data streams (binary and continuous) improving robustness/accuracy of inference, maintaining scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a linear relationship between log-transformed response times in the SPIRL task and participants’ uncertainty about the outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a linear relationship between log-transformed response times in the SPIRL task and participants' uncertainty about the outcome. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same empirical relationship but with reduced expressed certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adopting Bayesian workflow for generative modelling increases the transparency and robustness of results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Adopting Bayesian workflow for generative modeling increases the transparency and robustness of results in TN/CP. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the domain (Translational Neuromodeling/Computational Psychiatry) and expresses the claim more strongly (higher certainty), so semantic content is clarified and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel set of response models allows for simultaneous inference from multivariate behavioural data types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models that utilize information from both binary responses and continuous response times provide robust inference in behavioral data analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the functional outcome of such response models (robust inference using binary and continuous data) rather than emphasizing novelty; the claim is therefore clarified to focus on performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GABA concentration influences local recurrent inhibitory intrinsic connectivity in deep and superficial cortical layers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GABA concentration influences local recurrent inhibitory intrinsic connectivity in deep and superficial cortical layers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim with identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Glutamate influences the excitatory connections between superficial and deep layers and connections from superficial to inhibitory interneurons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Glutamate influences the excitatory connections between superficial and deep layers and connections from superficial to inhibitory interneurons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This glutamate-related connectivity result is preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hierarchical and empirical Bayesian framework is suitable for applications with magnetoencephalography or electroencephalography. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method is suitable for applications with magnetoencephalography or electroencephalography. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication carries the same methodological suitability claim for MEG/EEG with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method is well-suited to reveal the mechanisms of neurological and psychiatric disorders, including responses to psychopharmacological interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this specific broader translational claim about revealing mechanisms and drug responses; instead it emphasizes methodological reliability for model comparison, so the preprint's translational claim was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel time-varying dynamic network (TVDN) framework overcomes limitations of existing methods for extracting dynamic resting state functional connectivity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel time-varying dynamic network (TVDN) framework overcomes limitations of existing methods for extracting dynamic resting state functional connectivity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Text is effectively identical between preprint and publication, same contribution and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TVDN includes a generative model that relates low-dimensional dynamic resting state functional connectivity to brain signals. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TVDN includes a generative model that relates low-dimensional dynamic resting state functional connectivity to brain signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The statement is the same in content; only metadata label changed (contribution/result), so semantically unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Results demonstrate that TVDN can correctly capture the dynamics of brain activity and detect brain state switching more robustly in resting state fMRI and MEG data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Results demonstrate that TVDN is able to correctly capture the dynamics of brain activity and more robustly detect brain state switching in resting state fMRI and MEG data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Content and scope are the same; wording is slightly rephrased but claim is unchanged.

alignments.pub_claim_text: TVDN is applicable to multiple modalities of functional neuroimaging such as fMRI and MEG/EEG. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This application-to-multiple-modalities claim appears in the publication but is not present in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pairwise Potts models can extract information about the three-dimensional structure of folded proteins and the effect of amino-acid substitutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pairwise Potts models can extract information about the three-dimensional structure of folded proteins and the effect of amino acid substitutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and meaning are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach to parameter reduction based on selecting collective sequence motifs leads to the formulation of statistical sequence models in terms of Hopfield-Potts models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach to parameter reduction leads to the formulation of statistical sequence models in terms of Hopfield-Potts models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Substantively the same contribution; minor wording differences do not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using 20-40 Hopfield patterns is sufficient to obtain statistically close-to-generative models when applied to protein data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Even 20-40 patterns are sufficient to obtain statistically close-to-generative models when applied to protein data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric claim and meaning match closely between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The distributed collective nature of Hopfield patterns limits the ability of Hopfield-Potts models in predicting contact maps. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The distributed collective nature of Hopfield patterns limits the ability of Hopfield-Potts models in predicting contact maps. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Identical limitation claim retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relative powers of direct (D-INT) and indirect (I-INT) INT-based association tests depend on the underlying data generating process. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relative powers of direct (D-INT) and indirect (I-INT) INT-based association tests depend on the underlying data generating process. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is replicated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neither the direct (D-INT) nor the indirect (I-INT) approach is uniformly most powerful. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neither the direct (D-INT) nor the indirect (I-INT) approach is uniformly most powerful. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion appears unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adaptive omnibus test (O-INT) is robust to model misspecification, protects the type I error, and is well powered against a wide range of non-normally distributed traits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adaptive omnibus test (O-INT) is robust to model misspecification, protects the type I error, and is well powered against a wide range of nonnormally distributed traits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same methodological performance claims are retained; only minor punctuation/style changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: INT-based tests outperform the standard untransformed association test (UAT) for non-normally distributed traits in terms of power and type I error rate control. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: INT-based tests outperform the standard untransformed association test for nonnormally distributed traits in terms of power and type I error rate control. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance comparison claim is preserved with equivalent meaning.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed methods have been applied to GWAS of spirometry traits in the UK Biobank. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Application to UK Biobank GWAS appears in the publication but is not present in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The WGAN-GP model was applied to de novo promoter sequence design, generating entirely new promoter sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A novel AI-based framework for de novo promoter design in Escherichia coli was developed. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication reframes the specific WGAN-GP application as a novel AI framework and explicitly restricts to E. coli, clarifying and narrowing the original framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Out of 83 model-generated promoter sequences, 26 were found functional and successfully expressed with varying activities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Up to 70.8% of the AI-designed promoters were experimentally demonstrated to be functional after optimization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric outcome changes and the publication conditions include optimization; the success rate is higher but under different (optimized) conditions, so the claim is revised and its apparent effectiveness amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three of the newly designed promoters exhibited higher strength than wild type promoters and their highly expression mutants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding claim about three promoters exceeding wild type strength appears among the publication claims, so this specific result was removed from the claimed highlights.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model-designed novel promoters demonstrated a much higher success rate and promoter activity with lower similarity scores compared to natural promoters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI-designed promoters showed few significant sequence similarities with the E. coli genome. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the sequence-similarity aspect but does not fully preserve the combined claim about higher success rate and activity; thus the claim is narrowed to similarity and presented with more tempered certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings indicate the potential for deep generative models to be applied in genetic element designing in the future. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work indicates the potential to apply deep learning methods to de novo genetic element design. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the forward-looking conclusion using 'deep learning' terminology; the overall conclusion and confidence are preserved with clarified phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lower accuracy is seen in cross-study validation (CSV) than in traditional cross-validation (CV). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lower accuracy is seen in cross-study validation (CSV) than in traditional cross-validation (CV). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact result reiterated in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heterogeneity in known clinical covariates and differences in gene covariance structure have very limited contributions in the loss of accuracy when validating in new studies. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heterogeneity in known clinical covariates and differences in gene covariance structure have very limited contributions in the loss of accuracy when validating in new studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Substantive claim preserved; publication increases stated certainty but the claim content is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Forcing identical generative models greatly reduces the within/across study difference. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Forcing identical generative models greatly reduces the within/across study difference. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content matches; framing/labeling differs slightly but meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most easily identifiable sources of study heterogeneity are not necessarily the primary ones that undermine the ability to accurately replicate the accuracy of omics prediction models in new studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most easily identifiable sources of study heterogeneity are not necessarily the primary ones that undermine the ability to accurately replicate the accuracy of omics prediction models in new studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical conclusion retained in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unidentified heterogeneity, such as could arise from unmeasured confounding, may be more important. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unidentified heterogeneity, such as could arise from unmeasured confounding, may be more important. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The tentative conclusion about unidentified heterogeneity is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PID controller was an effective model of participants’ decisions in noisy, changing environments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PID controller was an effective model of participants' decisions in noisy, changing environments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim text and emphasis are essentially identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants’ behavior incorporated elements of PID updating in a change-point detection experiment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants' behavior incorporated elements of PID updating in a change-point detection experiment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result with the same framing and certainty; no substantive change is evident.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PID model offered better descriptions of behavioral adjustments than both the classical delta-rule model and the Kalman filter. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PID model offered better descriptions of behavioral adjustments than both the classical delta-rule model and the Kalman filter. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This comparative result is stated identically in the publication and retains the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants weighted different PID terms in response to salient environmental events, modulated by reward, surprise, and outcome entropy. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not report the modulation of PID term weighting by reward, surprise, or entropy, so this specific result appears to have been dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adaptive behavior in dynamic environments resembles PID control. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Adaptive learning in dynamic environments resembles PID control. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The high-level conclusion is retained with only minor wording changes ('behavior' vs 'learning'), preserving meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model achieved superior performance on challenging tasks designed to approximate real-world noise and occlusion viewing conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model achieved superior performance on challenging tasks designed to approximate real-world noise and occlusion viewing conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is restated in the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model better accounted for human behavioral reaction times and error patterns than a standard feedforward Convolutional Neural Network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model better accounted for human behavioral reaction times and error patterns than a standard feedforward Convolutional Neural Network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This behavioral-comparison result is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A complete understanding of object perception and recognition requires integrating top-down and attention feedback, which we propose is an object reconstruction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A complete understanding of object perception and recognition requires integrating top-down and attention feedback. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the high-level claim about integrating top-down feedback but omits the specific proposal that this takes the form of 'object reconstruction', narrowing the original conceptual claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The process of generating objects—actively reconstructing the most plausible object representation from noisy visual input—guides attention towards specific features or locations within an image. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The visual system actively facilitates recognition by reconstructing the object hypothesized to be in the image. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert active reconstruction plays a role, but the publication shifts emphasis from attention guidance to facilitation of recognition, a modest conceptual revision rather than deletion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AMPidentifer is a lightweight AI pipeline developed to discover biocompatible antimicrobial peptides from microbiomes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims focus on specific AMP1 results and an interdisciplinary screening approach; it does not assert the named 'AMPidentifer' pipeline as a standalone claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The selected two AMPs demonstrated good antimicrobial effects in vitro and in vivo. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AMP1 showed potent antibacterial and wound healing effects in mice, with comparable performances to vancomycin. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports strong in vivo effects but focuses on a single AMP (AMP1) rather than two AMPs as claimed in the preprint, narrowing the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: New efficient AMPs with low cytotoxicity were identified from the gut microbiome of Blattella germanica. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AMP1, derived from the symbiotic microorganism Blattabacterium cuenoti, demonstrated broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and low cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms identification of an AMP with low cytotoxicity from a Blattella-associated microbe but describes a specific AMP (AMP1) rather than multiple new AMPs, narrowing the preprint's plural claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Cys residue was demonstrated to perform different action mechanisms in the antimicrobial activity of the two AMPs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim describes differential roles of a Cys residue across two AMPs; mechanistic claims in the paper focus on AMP1's membrane permeation and intracellular accumulation.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Mechanistic studies revealed that AMP1 rapidly permeates the bacterial cell and accumulates intracellularly, resulting in a gradual and mild depolarization of the cell membrane. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This detailed mechanistic result about AMP1 is not present among the preprint's explicit claims and thus appears as an added publication claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This study exemplifies an interdisciplinary approach to screening safe and effective AMPs from natural biological tissues. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication draws a general methodological/conceptual conclusion not explicitly claimed in the preprint claims list, so it is treated as an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepRNA-Twist is a novel deep learning framework designed to predict RNA torsion and pseudo-torsion angles directly from sequence. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepRNA-Twist is a novel deep learning framework designed to predict RNA torsion and pseudo-torsion angles directly from sequence. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The core contribution claim is repeated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepRNA-Twist utilizes RNA language model embeddings to provide rich, context-aware feature representations of RNA sequences. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims highlight architectural elements (dilated convolutions, multi-head attention) but do not state the use of RNA language model embeddings, so this specific contribution is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepRNA-Twist demonstrated significant improvements over existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on benchmark datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepRNA-Twist demonstrated significant improvements over existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance/result claim is preserved in the publication with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The dilated convolutions in DeepRNA-Twist capture long-range dependencies in the sequence without requiring a large number of parameters. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This architectural explanation is not in the preprint claims list and appears as an added explanatory claim in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The multi-head attention mechanism in DeepRNA-Twist enhances the model's ability to focus on both local and global structural features simultaneously. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific claim about the role of multi-head attention that was not in the preprint's listed claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep neuronal network model (deepC) accurately predicts chromatin interactions from DNA sequence at megabase scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepC accurately predicts genome folding from megabase-scale DNA sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the main predictive result in equivalent terms, preserving scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepC generalizes well to unseen chromosomes and works across various cell types, Hi-C data resolutions, and sequencing depths. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: None of the publication claims explicitly state the broad generalization across unseen chromosomes, cell types, resolutions, and depths, indicating this specific claim was not preserved in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model integrates DNA sequence context on an unprecedented scale, bridging different levels of resolution from base pairs to TADs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepC predicts domain boundaries at high resolution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint framed the contribution broadly as bridging scales; the publication emphasizes a concrete high-resolution boundary-prediction capability, narrowing the original broad contribution into a specific predictive outcome.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepC allows for the investigation of sequence determinants of chromatin folding at a genome-wide scale. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepC learns the sequence determinants of genome folding. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements concern sequence determinants; the publication reframes this as an explicit result (learning determinants), which clarifies the preprint's investigative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts the importance of regulatory elements and the impact of sequence variations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepC predicts the impact of both large-scale structural and single base-pair variations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this capability with slightly expanded specificity (including single base-pair variations), which clarifies and slightly broadens the original phrasing while preserving the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stress induction increased blood pressure and psychological stress measures across groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stress induction increased blood pressure and psychological stress measures across groups and resulted in blunted cortisol responses in patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the preprint's stress and blood pressure result but adds patient cortisol findings, thereby clarifying and broadening the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with OCD exhibited blunted cortisol responses following stress induction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stress induction increased blood pressure and psychological stress measures across groups and resulted in blunted cortisol responses in patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states the same blunted cortisol response in patients; the content is preserved though presented combined with other results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a blunted reduction in connectivity between the caudate nucleus and precuneus in OCD patients after stress, which correlated positively with compulsivity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients showed a blunted reduction in connectivity between the caudate nucleus and precuneus during psychological distress, which was positively correlated with compulsivity but not obsession severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the connectivity–compulsivity relationship but adds the negative finding regarding obsession severity and presents it with higher certainty, narrowing and clarifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The posterior putamen showed no significant group differences in stress-induced connectivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the null finding about posterior putamen; the claim is not reported in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Compulsivity in OCD in the aftermath of stress is associated with altered connectivity between the goal-directed and default mode networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Compulsivity in OCD is associated with altered connectivity between the goal-directed and default mode networks during psychological distress. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion, with only minor wording ("during psychological distress" vs "aftermath of stress"), so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Observers’ Glx concentration is significantly higher while GABA concentration is significantly lower when viewing mixed-polarity random-dot stereograms than when viewing single-polarity random-dot stereograms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants' Glx concentration is significantly higher when mixed-polarity random-dot stereograms are viewed than when single-polarity random-dot stereograms are viewed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication splits the combined neurochemical result into separate statements; the Glx component matches directly and is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Observers’ Glx concentration is significantly higher while GABA concentration is significantly lower when viewing mixed-polarity random-dot stereograms than when viewing single-polarity random-dot stereograms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants' GABA concentration is significantly lower when mixed-polarity random-dot stereograms are viewed than when single-polarity random-dot stereograms are viewed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: As above, the GABA component is presented separately in the publication; the original combined result is preserved across the two pub claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Excitation and inhibition facilitate processing of single- and mixed-polarity stereograms in the early visual cortex to different extents. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Excitation and inhibition facilitate processing of single- and mixed-polarity stereograms in the early visual cortex to different extents. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this mechanistic conclusion with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mixed-polarity benefit is supported by evidence that single- and mixed-polarity stereograms evoke different levels of positive and negative activity in a deep neural network. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim references the deep neural network evidence; that specific contribution is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have built a first draft statistical model of the neuron-to-neuron micro-connectome of a whole mouse neocortex. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A first draft statistical model of the micro-connectome of a whole mouse neocortex has been built using available data on region-to-region connectivity and individual whole-brain axon reconstructions. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with consistent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This process revealed a novel targeting principle that allowed us to predict the innervation logic of individual axons from meso-scale data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The process reveals a targeting principle that allows for the prediction of the innervation logic of individual axons from meso-scale data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same targeting-principle result with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resulting micro-connectome of 10 million neurons and 88 billion synapses recreates biological trends of targeting on the macro-meso- and micro-scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting connectome recreates biological trends of targeting on all scales and predicts that an established principle of scale invariant topological organization of connectivity can be extended down to the level of individual neurons. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the recreation claim and adds an explicit prediction about extending scale-invariant organization to single neurons, broadening the interpretive scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This openly accessible connectome can serve as a powerful null model to compare experimental findings to. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model can serve as a powerful null model and as a substrate for whole-brain simulations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the null-model role and explicitly adds an additional use as a substrate for simulations, broadening the utility claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We provide a set of 1,854 diverse object concepts sampled systematically from concrete picturable and nameable nouns in the American English language. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We provide a set of 1,854 diverse object concepts sampled systematically from concrete picturable and nameable nouns in the American English language. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this contribution verbatim; no substantive change is evident.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We compiled a database of 26,107 high-quality naturalistic images of those objects, with 12 or more object images per concept. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We compiled a database of 26,107 high-quality naturalistic images of those objects, with 12 or more object images per concept. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate that images fed through a deep convolutional neural network exhibit high selectivity for different object concepts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate that images fed through a deep convolutional neural network exhibit high selectivity for different object concepts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is presented identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The THINGS database offers a tool for both systematic and large-scale naturalistic research in the fields of psychology, neuroscience, and computer science. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The THINGS database offers a tool for both systematic and large-scale naturalistic research in the fields of psychology, neuroscience, and computer science. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same concluding statement about the database's utility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model accounts for all fundamental qualitative and quantitative features of saccadic countermanding, including neuronal activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model accounts for all fundamental qualitative and quantitative features of saccadic countermanding, including neuronal activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this core model result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Saccadic inhibition (SI) accounts for a large proportion of the saccade countermanding process when using visual signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Saccadic inhibition (SI) accounts for a large proportion of the saccade countermanding process when using visual signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This empirical claim is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Top-down inhibition acts later, piggy-backing on the quicker automatic inhibition. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Top-down inhibition acts later, piggy-backing on the quicker automatic inhibition. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same temporal relationship claim but reports it with lower stated certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This conceptualization casts different light on the concept of top-down inhibition, its timing and neural underpinning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This conceptualization fully accounts for the known effects of signal features and response modalities traditionally used across the countermanding literature. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the conceptual point into a stronger claim that the model fully accounts for known effects across the literature, broadening and revising the original conceptual statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interpretation of stop-signal reaction time is affected by the findings presented in this study. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state that interpretation of stop-signal reaction time is affected; that specific interpretive claim is not present among the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computer-based analysis technique based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) achieves a superior cross-validation accuracy of 83.7% and testing accuracy of 80% for diagnosing Parkinson's disease compared to traditional methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed computer-based analysis technique using convolutional neural networks achieves a testing accuracy of 80% for diagnosing Parkinson's disease from NMS-MRI.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same 80% test accuracy but omits the cross-validation statistic and the explicit comparative phrasing, making the claim more specific and narrower in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The contrast ratio-based classification method has significantly lower cross-validation and testing accuracies of 52.7% and 56.5%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural networks technique outperforms traditional contrast ratio-based classification, which has a testing accuracy of 56.5%.
alignments.rationale: Both specify the 56.5% testing accuracy for the contrast method and the CNN outperforming it; the publication omits the cross-validation number, narrowing the presented evidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed technique identifies the left substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) as playing a key role in the classification of Parkinson's disease, supporting the concept of asymmetry in the disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis identifies the left substantia nigra pars compacta as playing a key role in the classification of Parkinson's disease, supporting the concept of asymmetry in the disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about the left SNc and disease asymmetry with essentially identical wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new technique has the potential to enhance radiological diagnosis of Parkinson's disease and improve understanding of abnormalities in the substantia nigra pars compacta.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the broader translational contribution (potential to enhance radiological diagnosis / understanding) present in the preprint, so this contribution claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Alpha peak frequency systematically decreased over time, while alpha-power increased.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Alpha peak frequency systematically decreased over time, while alpha-power increased.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Statistical source separation revealed that some alpha components displayed concomitant power increases and peak frequency decreases, while others showed either unique power increases or frequency decreases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Statistical source separation revealed that some alpha components displayed concomitant power increases and peak frequency decreases, while others showed either unique power increases or frequency decreases.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding with the same level of detail and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Components that showed mixed frequency/power changes oscillated primarily in the lower alpha-band, while components with unique changes oscillated primarily in the higher alpha-band.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Components that showed mixed frequency/power changes oscillated primarily in the lower alpha-band (∼8-10 Hz), while components with unique changes oscillated primarily in the higher alpha-band (∼9-13 Hz).
alignments.rationale: The publication adds explicit numeric frequency ranges, making the original categorical band statement more specific (narrower) without changing the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings provide novel clues on the time-varying intrinsic properties of large-scale neural networks as measured by M/EEG.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings provide novel clues on the time-varying intrinsic properties of large-scale neural networks as measured by M/EEG.
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NeoDTI demonstrates substantial prediction performance improvement over other state-of-the-art DTI prediction methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NeoDTI demonstrates substantial prediction performance improvement over other state-of-the-art DTI prediction methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the result claim identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NeoDTI has superior predictive power supported by several novel predicted DTIs with evidence from previous studies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NeoDTI has superior predictive power supported by several novel predicted DTIs with evidence from previous studies.
alignments.rationale: This predictive-power claim is carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NeoDTI is robust against a wide range of choices of hyperparameters.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NeoDTI is robust against a wide range of choices of hyperparameters.
alignments.rationale: The robustness claim is preserved verbatim (though labeled as 'result' in the publication).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NeoDTI can integrate more drug and target related information, such as compound-protein binding affinity data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NeoDTI can integrate more drug and target related information, such as compound-protein binding affinity data.
alignments.rationale: The integration capability claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NeoDTI can offer a powerful and robust tool for drug development and drug repositioning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NeoDTI can offer a powerful and robust tool for drug development and drug repositioning.
alignments.rationale: The translational claim is reproduced without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The context-induced feeding (ctx-IF) task can lead to increased food consumption in sated mice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The context-induced feeding (Ctx-IF) task can lead to increased food consumption in sated mice.
alignments.rationale: The main behavioral finding is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spaced training is more effective than massed training in the ctx-IF task.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spaced training is more effective than massed training in the context-induced feeding task.
alignments.rationale: The result is the same, but the publication reports it with lower stated certainty (preprint=high, publication=medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The insular cortex and other brain regions are activated following exposure to cues denoting the availability of food.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies specific brain regions, including the insular cortex, that are activated during the context-induced feeding task.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report activation of the insular cortex and other regions during the task; the publication frames this as an identification of specific regions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inhibition of the insular cortex using GABA agonists impairs performance of the ctx-IF task.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inhibition of the insular cortex using GABA agonists impairs performance of the context-induced feeding task.
alignments.rationale: The causal manipulation and its behavioral effect are preserved with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ctx-IF task provides a novel assay for defining the functional neuroanatomy of appetitive conditioning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies specific brain regions, including the insular cortex, that are activated during the context-induced feeding task.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly claim the task as a 'novel assay' but reports identification of brain regions (a narrower, more specific demonstration), and the publication states this with stronger certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In neural networks formed in mature hippocampal cultures with high cellular density, the spiking activity self-organized and converged to long, complex and rhythmically repeated superbursts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Self-organized spiking activity in mature hippocampal cultures converges to long, complex, and rhythmically repeated superbursts.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this primary result with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The superburst lasted tens of seconds and consisted of hundreds of short bursts with a high spiking rate of 139.0 ± 78.6 Hz.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The superburst lasts for tens of seconds and consists of hundreds of short bursts with a high spiking rate of 139.0 ± 78.6 Hz.
alignments.rationale: The detailed quantitative description of the superburst is preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interval between peak burst activities in the range of 100-150 ms can be treated as a theta rhythm (11.2 ± 1.5 Hz).
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the explicit interpretation of the inter-burst interval as a theta rhythm with the given frequency, so this particular interpretation appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The long superburst can be classified into two types, each associated with a unique direction of spike propagation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The long superburst is classified into two types, each associated with a unique direction of spike propagation.
alignments.rationale: The classification into two propagation-associated superburst types is retained unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The precisely structured bidirectional rhythmic activity developed in self-organizing cultured networks was similar to what was observed in in vivo experiments.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state the claimed similarity to in vivo observations; instead it emphasizes internal motifs, so the comparative claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cerebellar patients showed a learning-related increase in activity in the parahippocampal cortex and cerebellar Crus I. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cerebellar patients showed a learning-related increase in activity in the parahippocampal cortex and cerebellar Crus I. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Dynamic causal modeling analysis revealed that modulation of M1 to cerebellum and putamen to cerebellum connections were significantly more negative for sequence compared to random blocks in controls. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural network changes were associated with learning performance in patients, with learners showing a negative modulation from right SMA to right M1 similar to controls. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results highlight the role of the cerebellum in motor learning and demonstrate its functional role as part of the cortico-striato-cerebellar network. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results highlight the role of the cerebellum in motor learning and demonstrate its functional role as part of the cortico-striato-cerebellar network. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Cortical thickness increased across sensory processing hierarchies, primarily driven by layers III, V, and VI. (certainty=high, type=result)
